name: Antikythera Mechanism
description: The Antikythera Mechanism is an ancient Greek hand-powered orrery, described as the oldest example of an analogue computer used to predict astronomical positions and eclipses decades in advance. Discovered in 1901 in the Antikythera shipwreck off the Greek island of Antikythera, between Kythera and Crete, it has been dated to around the 2nd century BC. Proponents of alternative theories suggest it demonstrates advanced technological capabilities far exceeding what is traditionally attributed to the ancient Greeks, implying lost knowledge or even extraterrestrial influence. These claims often highlight the complexity of the mechanism's gearing and its ability to predict celestial events with surprising accuracy. Mainstream scholarship acknowledges the mechanism's sophistication but firmly places it within the context of Hellenistic Greek science and engineering. Researchers emphasize that while the mechanism is remarkable, it does not necessarily indicate a widespread loss of technology or evidence of alien intervention. Rather, it represents a pinnacle of achievement within a specific historical period, building upon existing knowledge of astronomy, mathematics, and metallurgy. The device is thought to have been used for calendrical calculations, astronomical predictions, and possibly astrological purposes. Ongoing research continues to decipher the remaining fragments and refine our understanding of its construction and function, revealing more about the ingenuity and scientific capabilities of the ancient Greeks. The debate centers around the interpretation of its complexity – whether it is a unique artifact or evidence of a more advanced, now-lost civilization.
subtitle: Ancient Greek Astronomical Calculator

claims.claim: The Antikythera Mechanism proves advanced, lost technology.
claims.evidence: Its intricate gearing and ability to predict astronomical events.
claims.criticism: Mainstream view attributes it to Hellenistic Greek ingenuity within their scientific context.

claims.claim: The mechanism suggests knowledge of technology beyond conventional understanding of ancient Greece.
claims.evidence: The precision and complexity of its construction.
claims.criticism: While complex, it aligns with known Greek advancements in astronomy, mathematics, and engineering.

claims.claim: The device points to possible extraterrestrial influence on ancient Greek technology.
claims.evidence: The perceived sophistication exceeding known Greek capabilities.
claims.criticism: Lack of any credible evidence to support extraterrestrial involvement; simpler explanations exist.

claims.claim: The mechanism was used for astrological predictions.
claims.evidence: Some markings on the device seem to correspond to astrological signs.
claims.criticism: While possible, the primary function was likely calendrical and astronomical, with astrological applications being secondary or speculative.

keyFigures.name: Derek J. de Solla Price
keyFigures.role: Early researcher who recognized the mechanism's significance

keyFigures.name: Michael Wright
keyFigures.role: Reconstructed a working model of the mechanism

keyFigures.name: Tony Freeth
keyFigures.role: Led the Antikythera Mechanism Research Project using modern imaging techniques

keyFigures.name: Yanis Bitsakis
keyFigures.role: Physicist and historian of science who contributed to understanding the mechanism's purpose

mythologyConnections: Connections to celestial gods and myths

timeline.event: First studies and initial recognition of its complexity
Advanced imaging and reconstruction efforts leading to a better understanding of its function
